result:
**Merge** preserves history as-is. It creates a new merge commit that ties two branch tips together, keeping both parent lineages intact. Non-destructive, but produces a branching graph.

**Rebase** rewrites history. It replays your branch's commits on top of another base, producing a linear sequence. Each replayed commit gets a new SHA — the originals are effectively replaced.

Key differences:

| | Merge | Rebase |
|---|---|---|
| History shape | Branching graph | Linear |
| Commit SHAs | Preserved | Rewritten |
| Creates extra commit | Yes (merge commit) | No |
| Safe on shared branches | Yes | No — force-push required, disrupts collaborators |
| Conflict resolution | Once, in the merge commit | Potentially per replayed commit |

Rule of thumb: **merge** for shared/public branches, **rebase** for cleaning up local work before pushing.
